titleStmt.title: Memorandum by Mr. W. G. MacLean of the Division of the American Republics

head.hi: Memorandum by Mr. W. G. MacLean of the Division of the American Republics
head.note: Addressed to the Chief of the Division of the American Republics (Bonsal), the Assistant Chief of that Division (McGurk), and the Assistant Adviser on International Economic Affairs (Mulliken).

div.p: In the underlying despatch no. 10915 of June 21, 1943,Not printed. the Embassy reports in paragraph 3 that the Mexican Government has issued orders to its Consuls in Texas that no Mexican agricultural workers should be sent into that State under existing agreements because of the prevalence of discrimination there against Mexican residents.

This situation has been rumored for some time but is only now confirmed in connection with special efforts made by Judge BriggsJudge Cullen Briggs, Nueces County, Texas. of Texas in Mexico City to arrange for workers for Texas. Foreign Minister Padilla said that if the situation with respect to discrimination in Texas improved, he would be glad to have the attitude of the Mexican Government reconsidered.

Judge Briggs told the Mexican Government and our Embassy that he would take the matter up with Governor Stevenson.Governor Coke R. Stevenson of Texas. One step suggested by Judge Briggs was the writing of a letter on the subject by Governor Stevenson to the appropriate Mexican officials, and a draft copy thereof was prepared with the counsel of the Embassy.

A step taken by the Governor, at the insistence of Judge Briggs, according to airgram A–57 of June 26 from Consul General Blocker,Airgram not printed. was a proclamation on the Good Neighbor Policy dated June 25 for publication in all Texas papers. Attached is a clipping from La Prensa of San Antonio of June 27 which contains the Governor’s proclamation, which turns out to be the resolution passed by the Texas legislature and signed by the Governor on May 6, last, and which was the subject of RA–Me’sMexican section of the Division of the American Republics. memorandum of June 26, 1943, a copy of which is attached.Not found in Department files.

The Ambassador invites attention to the fact that so far no Mexican agricultural workers have been certified for Texas, and that such certification if made from now on will raise this worker-racial discrimination question in concrete form. It is therefore interesting to note that Judge Briggs approached the War Food Administration yesterday to take up the question of securing workers.
